title: Funding Liquidity without Banks: Evidence from a Shock to the Cost of Very Short-Term Debt
abstract: In 2011, Colombia instituted a tax on repayment of bank loans, which increased the cost of short-term bank credit more than long-term credit. Firms responded by cutting short-term loans for liquidity management purposes and increasing the use of cash and trade credit. In industries in which trade credit is more accessible (based on U.S. Compustat firms), we find substitution into accounts payable and little effect on cash and investment. Where trade credit is less available, firms increase cash and cut investment. Thus, trade credit provides an alternative source of liquidity that can insulate some firms from bank liquidity shocks.

article.introduction:
How do firms manage liquidity? Most of the economics and finance literature focuses on the banking system, with banks acting as the primary supplier of liquidity and payment mechanisms between counterparties. Cash, demand deposits, and credit lines—or equivalently, very short-term bank loans—are fundamental payment products supplied almost uniquely by banks (Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002), Gatev and Strahan (2009)). Many firms use credit lines to smooth out liquidity needs over time, drawing funds when payments need to be made (e.g., to make payroll or to pay for supplies) and repaying those funds when payments are received (e.g., after realizing sales receipts). Demand deposits and credit lines differ mainly in that the latter include a credit aspect (they are not prefunded by the customer), whereas the former do not. Firms with limited access to lines of credit use deposits (out of their buffer stock of cash) to make payments.1
In this paper, we analyze how firms’ liquidity management changes following an exogenous increase in the relative price of very short-term bank debt. We find that some firms substitute into cash, whereas others substitute into trade credit as an alternative source of liquidity. In industries in which trade credit is less available, the increase in cash is large. As a result, these firms cut both long-term and short-term investment. In contrast, in industries with greater access to trade credit, firms increase net accounts payable, but neither their cash balances nor their investment change after the shock. Taken together, these results lead to two implications. First, using cash can be a costly substitute for bank liquidity facilities. When external funds are costlier than internal sources of financing, firms that need to increase their stock of cash (because short-term bank credit has become expensive) must do so by drawing funds away from investment.2Second, for firms in some industries, trade credit provides liquidity services that can substitute for bank-provided liquidity without distorting real decisions.
To conduct our analysis, we consider the empirical setting of Colombia, whose two unique institutional features allow us to identify how an increase in the cost—or equivalently, a decrease in the supply—of bank liquidity affects firms. First, in Colombia, “preferential clients,” that is, clients with annual sales above a specific sales cutoff set by banks, have greater access to bank credit than do smaller firms. In particular, these preferential clients have access to Treasury Facilities—very short-term bank loans—which they can use to manage their liquidity needs but not to finance real investment due to the facilities’ very short maturity.3Second, in 2011 Colombia adopted a new law that taxes each payment on a bank loan. This regulatory change made short-maturity Treasury Facilities (and other very short-term debt instruments) prohibitively expensive, but had little effect on the cost of long-term bank loans (due to the low tax rate of 0.4%). Given these two features, we focus our empirical analysis on large firms with access to Treasury Facilities, and construct a difference-in-differences identification strategy to exploit variation in this access, as heavy users of Treasury Facilities are expected to be most affected by the tax change. Firms with access to Treasury Facilities (as proxied by preregulatory change use of short-term bank debt issuance with original maturity less than 60 days) constitute our treatment sample; firms without such access are taken to be the control sample.
We report three core results. First, the use of short-term bank borrowing for liquidity falls sharply after 2011 for firms exposed to the tax shock. Second, the decline in short-term bank borrowing leads to a substitution into both trade credit and cash. In particular, both net accounts payable and cash increase after 2011, with the increases being larger for firms exposed to the tax shock. Third, the decline in bank-supplied liquidity leads to a decrease in both long-term and short-term investment. Specifically, capital expenditures and a proxy for investment in research and development (R&D) both decline for some treated firms. In addition, short-term inventory investment in raw materials and unfinished goods declines. Across industries, however, those with high access to trade credit substitute into accounts payable and experience no investment declines. Moreover, the substitution to trade credit is complete in the sense that there isnochange in cash for firms in these sectors. In contrast, firms in industries with greater frictions in their ability to borrow from suppliers experience no change in accounts payable but a large increase in cash and a large decrease in investment (in both long-term and short-term assets). Thus, in these segments the decline in bank liquidity spills over to real decisions. A decline in long-term investment likely occurs because firms need to divert funds to increase their buffer stock of cash to manage liquidity. This increase in cash displaces investment. Whether this decline is permanent is difficult to determine, given our short time window of just four years following treatment. The effect on inventories, however, is more direct because firms use short-term bank debt (along with trade credit) as a key source of funds for investments in working capital.4
As we describe in SectionI, the tax regime in Colombia distorts the relative after-tax prices of tools that firms use to manage liquidity—bank credit facilities, trade credit, and cash—in the pre- and post-2011 periods. Our paper thus compares two equilibria. In the first regime (pre-2011), many firms minimize taxes by using relatively tax-advantaged very short-term bank debt, rather than cash or trade credit, to smooth payments. In this regime, efforts to avoid the tax distort firm behavior by encouraging firms to use short-term debt. In the second regime (after 2011), the tax change reduces this distortion by increasing the relative price of short-term bank debt (so that its tax treatment becomes the same as that on the other payment mechanisms). This increase leads to a sharp decline in the use of short-term bank debt and in turn to an increase in the use of liquidity substitutes, namely, cash and trade credit. The increased demand for trade credit leads to a higher price because domestic trade credit suppliers sometimes use bank credit to finance their accounts receivable (which is therefore also indirectly affected by the tax shock).5Hence, only firms with good access to trade credit as a source of funds increase its use, whereas other firms instead increase their use of cash. Increasing the cash buffer leads to investment distortions, whereas increased use of trade credit (i.e., accounts payable) does not.6
In contrast to the extant literature, our shock focuses on the liquidity role of banks. Most existing studies test how shocks that affect banks’ cost of funds (e.g., monetary policy), bank solvency (e.g., bank failures, as in Peek and Rosengren (2000)), or the supply of local bank deposits (e.g., Becker (2007), Gilje, Loutskina, and Strahan (2016)) propagate to nonfinancial firms. These types of shocks affect both the liquidity/payments role of banks and the credit-production role, thus making it hard to distinguish which aspect of banking (credit or liquidity) affects which firm outcomes. We know of no existing study that can isolate a system-wide shock to the payments/liquidity role alone. As we explain below, the tax shock in our setting changes the relative prices of short-term bank credit versus other payment mechanisms, but has little effect on the cost of longer term bank credit. Thus, it has little effect on the credit role of the banking sector. We find that trade credit can substitute for the liquidity-provision role of banks in some industries. This result is consistent with Fisman and Love (2003), who show that poor financial development constrains growth less in industries that can substitute toward trade credit. Our setting differs, however, because we consider an exogenous shock to bank liquidity supply within a well-developed (bank-based) financial system.7
Our results suggest that investment can be distorted by shocks to bank-supplied liquidity. As noted above, most of the literature considers cases in which drawing a bright line between credit and liquidity is difficult. The 2007 to 2008 Financial Crisis illustrates how difficult such a separation can be, as solvency shocks metastasized when amplified by illiquidity, leading to a massive reduction in bank credit and liquidity, followed by an economic disaster (e.g., Brunnermeier (2009), Ivashina and Scharfstein (2010)). Much of the banking and macroeconomics literature focuses on the importance of banks as suppliers of liquidity and credit to firms, but do not separate the two roles.8As a specific example of how hard that separation can be, consider Merrouche and Nier (2012), who study a shock that improves bankpayment systems. The authors find that following payment innovations (in several Eastern European countries), funds flowed into banks, which used those funds in turn to expandcredit. Our empirical setting is again different because it allows us to focus on the liquidity role alone, with little feedback to the credit role of banks.9We find that many firms can obtain this liquidity from firm-to-firm trade credit without constraining their investment decisions.
We also contribute to the literature on trade credit by cleanly tracing out how firms respond to a well-identified increase in trade credit demand originating from a shock to their banks. Early studies, such as Petersen and Rajan (1997), rely on reduced-form links from firm characteristics to accounts payable and receivable, making it hard to distinguish demand-side from supply-side effects. More recent papers, in contrast, attempt to identify one side or the other. Amberg et al. (2016), for example, use the failure of Panaxia—a Swedish cash-in-transit firm—as a plausibly exogenous shock to liquidity demand for firms exposed to the failure. They find that this shock leads to an increase in the use of trade credit and a decline in firms’ cash balances. Using shocks to payment terms in the French trucking industry, Barrot (2016) finds that firms substitute into cash when trade credit supply declines. Using a similar restriction on trade credit terms among Chilean retailers, Breza and Lieberman (2017) also find a decline in trade credit accompanied by an increase in vertical integration.
A number of existing studies emphasize the relative substitutability of banks versus trade creditors. Both Wilner (2000) and Cunat (2007) argue that trade creditors have advantages over banks in enforcement and renegotiation. Burkart and Ellingsen (2004) further argue that trade creditors have an advantage over banks because they directly supply goods rather than cash, which unlike goods is uniquely vulnerable to diversion (i.e., theft). However, none of these studies trace out how shocks to banks affect trade credit, as we do. Similar to our study, Garcia-Appendini and Montoriol-Garriga (2013) find indirect evidence of substitution in that firms with relatively high cash extend more trade credit and constrained firms use more trade credit during the financial crisis. Interpretation is complicated, however, because high-cash firms were also better able to sustain investment following the crisis (Duchin, Ozbas, and Sensoy (2010)) and because the crisis affected bank liquidity and credit supply as well as investment demand. Our study differs in that we examine the decline in bank-supplied liquidity in response to a specific tax shock, as opposed to a broad credit shock like the crisis, with limited spillovers to potential confounds such as changing investment opportunities.
The reminder of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we describe our institutional setting. In SectionII, we discuss the research design and identification, summarize the data, and present the results. Finally, we conclude in SectionIII.
article.I. Colombian BAD Taxes, the 2011 Tax Cha:
Colombia offers an ideal setting to assess the importance of bank loans for firm liquidity management. First, the 2011 tax change that we describe below offers a clean shock to the relative cost of using bank credit for liquidity purposes. Second, Colombia is a bank-centric economy with limited alternatives for liquidity in other parts of the financial system; for instance, the commercial paper market that is available to large, public firms in the United States, is largely absent in Colombia. Third, we have access to detailed financial statements data on a large, representative sample of firms in Colombia, both before and after the tax shock, which allow us to observe within-firm changes in cash management, financial policies, and investment.
The 2011 tax shock emerged out of the bank account debit tax (BAD), which was adopted in 1999 amid a weak economy, an unhealthy financial system, and low fiscal revenues.10Under the original legislation, all debits from bank accounts generate a tax liability. Thus, BAD taxes affect all withdrawals from bank accounts, including check clearances, electronic transfers, ATM cash withdrawals, loan payments (until 2011, firms could avoid the tax on loan payments), and most other transactions that involve debiting money from a bank account.11
The tax rate started at 0.2% per transaction and increased to 0.3% in 2001. The rate increased again in 2004 to its current level of 0.4%. Over time, firms and households found ways to avoid the tax. For example, payments made outside the banking system are not subject to the BAD tax. Figure1shows the general effect of the advent of tax arbitrage by plotting BAD tax receipts (relative to GDP) over time. As economic agents learn how to avoid the tax, collections gradually decline. Indeed, Arbeláez, Burnman, and Zuluaga (2004) document a significant increase in the use of currency relative to bank deposits following the introduction of the tax, which is the easiest way to avoid it. They also document a decrease in the number of checks cleared after the tax, with checks often circulating for many payments and with multiple endorsements before finally clearing and being taxed. Restrepo (2019) shows that this disintermediation reduced the supply of bank credit, which reduced in turn financially dependent firms’ access to credit. Thus, consistent with most of the current banking literature, the original BAD tax affected both the liquidity and the credit roles of banks.
Prior to 2011, large firms could—and did—use Treasury Facilities (i.e., very short-term bank loans) to make payments without incurring the BAD tax. Payors would borrow funds and deposit those funds directly into the recipient's bank account, thereby avoiding a bank account withdrawal and the corresponding tax. Then, when the loan was due, firms would make the loan payment using an investment account, again avoiding paying the tax on the repayment. The 2011 tax law change stopped this practice.12As can be seen in Figure1, BAD tax receipts jumped by about one-third after the tax law change (from 0.59% to 0.82% of GDP in 2010 and 2011, respectively) without increasing the statutory tax rate, which has remained at 0.4% since 2004.
The easiest way to understand the effect of the tax change is with an example. Consider a firm purchasing $10 million in supplies. Payment options include (i) an immediate cash disbursement from funds withdrawn from the firm's checking account, (ii) the use of trade credit (with, say, up to 30 days to pay), or (iii) the use of funds borrowed from a Treasury Facility. Under the pre-2011 tax regime, the firm would pay a tax of $40,000 (0.4% times $10 million) using either cash or trade credit because either mechanism would require a debit of $10 million from the firm's checking account. The difference between the two mechanisms comes down to timing. With cash, payment would be immediate. With trade credit, a firm could make the payment at any time over the subsequent 30 days, which would allow the firm to time its payment such that it coincides with the firm's sales receipts, thus reducing the optimal balance in its checking account.13Note that the tax burden depends on the flow of payments through the checking account, not the level (or buffer stock) held in the account. However, before 2011 the firm could avoid the tax entirely by borrowing the $10 million from a Treasury Facility and having the payment deposited directly into the supplier's bank. Firms with access to these facilities would use them to avoid the debit transaction tax.
In 2011, the Colombian Government effectively eliminated the option of using funds borrowed from a bank, such as a Treasury Facility, to avoid the BAD tax. This occurred because under the regulatory change, banks can no longer deposit borrowed funds directly into a third party's account, and borrowers incur the tax when the borrowers’ funds are repaid to the bank. Thus, in the post-2011 tax regime, borrowing $10 million from the bank to make a payment would result in being assessed the bank debit tax twice, first when these funds go into the borrower's checking account (and hence incur the BAD tax when the funds are used to pay the supplier), and second when the loan comes due. Thus, following the regulatory change, firms are now better off either paying suppliers from cash (i.e., from their checking account) immediately, or using trade credit. In both cases, the firm would have to debit $10 million from its account to pay the supplier and thereby incur the same $40,000 tax (ignoring the small effect of the time value of funds). However, trade credit would have the advantage of allowing the firm to time its payments to coincide with incoming sales receipts. Accordingly, we expect firm demand for trade credit (i.e., accounts payable) toincreaseafter 2011, as this mechanism gives firms the ability to smooth payments and reduce its holdings of a cash buffer. Market clearing dictates that an increase in trade credit demand leads to an equivalent increase in trade credit supply, so we expect accounts receivable to increase after 2011 as well.
As we will show, the tax law change in 2011 did indeed encourage firms to switch away from bank loans for payments and increase the use of trade credit (or if not available, cash). However, as we also argue, the change had little effect on the cost of long-term credit from banks. In addition, the tax change had no effect on the other side of the banking system's balance sheet (hence there is no reason to expect the supply of longer term loans to change), nor did it have a large effect on the cost of using bank debt to finance investment directly.
Figure2illustrates this latter point. The figure graphs the increase in borrowing cost on an annualized basis as a function of a loan's maturity. Because borrowers must pay the 0.4% tax for every loan payment, it affects the “all-in” cost of debt most for very short-maturity loans. For instance, consider the annualized cost of a loan with APR of 8%, roughly the average rate for a bank loan in Colombia. At a maturity of five days, the all-in cost annualizes to 37.2% (= [(1 + 8.0% × 5/365) × (1 + 0.4%) – 1] × (365/5)). In contrast, the “all-in” cost for a one-year loan comes to just 8.4% (= [(1 + 8.0% × 365/365) * (1 + 0.4%) –1] × (365/365)). By the time maturity exceeds 60 days, the effect of the tax is small. At a maturity of one year, the tax component falls to just 0.4%, a 5% increase in the average interest rate on a bank loan in Colombia. For longer loans, the effect is even smaller.
Figure3shows that the tax change had a major impact on the use of bank liquidity. In particular, aggregate issuance of bank loans for liquidity management to preferential customers—Treasury Facilities—falls by about 80% precisely when the tax change went into effect.
We have argued that the 2011 change relates to liquidity, not credit. This can be seen in Figure4, where we graph Deposits/GDP and Credit/GDP for the Colombian banking system since 1990. The figure provides evidence of disintermediation in the years just following the introduction of the BAD tax (1999), and then recovery due to the advent of the tax avoidance strategies discussed earlier. Importantly there is no change in the trends of either deposits or credit right around the 2011 tax change—both are increasing in line with the overall expansion of the economy. This observation supports our claim that the 2011 tax affectedliquidity—that is, very short-term loans and credit lines—but had no noticeable impact on long-termcreditsupply.14
article.II. Research Design, Data and Results:
As explained above, we exploit two unique features of the Colombian financial system to construct our difference-in-differences models. First, we focus on firms with sales above the threshold of COP 15,000 million in the year prior to the shock, as these firms are the predominant users of Treasury Facilities. Smaller firms do sometimes use bank credit lines to make payments, but these are more limited in the amount committed and they come with tighter contractual restrictions (e.g., covenants) compared to the Treasury Facilities.15Second, we measure exposure to the tax change using an indicator equal to 1 for firms in the top quintile of the distribution of average pre-2011 use of very short-term bank credit (loans issued with original maturity under 60 days).16Figure5plots this distribution. As can be seen, short-term debt usage is nearly zero for about 40% of large firms and hence these firms are affected little by the 2011 tax change. Among firms with very short-term loans, short-term debt usage increases sharply in the uppermost quintile. We thus use firms in the top quintile as our treatment sample (inferring access to Treasury Facilities based on their high level of very short-term debt issuance).17
We compare the treated firms with two sets of control firms, which allow us to build two sets of difference-in-differences regressions. In the first, we include firms in the bottom two quintiles from Figure5. These firms serve as natural controls because they are roughly matched in terms of the size of sales yet have nearly zero very short-term bank debt. In the second, we construct a matched sample based on a propensity score estimated from a Probit regression that explains treatment status as a function of preperiod firm characteristics. The Probit sample contains all of the treated firms and all of the control firms from the first approach (i.e., all firms in the bottom two quintiles and the top quintile of Figure5). This model shows that larger and older firms are more likely to be treated, which suggests that much of the variation in short-term debt comes from better access for larger and older firms, which likely have longstanding reputations to protect with their banks.18
We perform nearest-neighbor matching with replacement. Although there is no clear rule in the literature for determining the number of matches, the choice implies a trade-off between bias and efficiency (Heinrich, Maffioli, and Vazquez (2010)). Choosing only one neighbor improves the precision of the estimator, but at the cost of efficiency. Using multiple neighbors increases efficiency, but at the cost of precision. We choose three matches as this number seems to offer a good balance where this trade-off is concerned.19To ensure that there is sufficient overlap in the characteristics of treatment and control firms, we also require that matches fall in the common support of estimated propensity scores (no firms are dropped due to this requirement). The matched control group, by construction, looks more similar along observables to firms in the control group prior to the tax change.
We include both firm (αi) and industry-time (γj,t) fixed effects in all models. The fixed effects capture unobserved, time-invariant heterogeneity across firms as well as industry-specific temporal shocks that affect all firms in the same industry. For instance, they absorb industry-specific business cycle effects, as well as any differential trends across industries. Because our cross-sectional measure of exposure to the tax is time-invariant, its direct effect is captured by the firm fixed effects. By using pre-2011 levels to defineTreatment, we avoid potential feedback from the tax change to firm outcomes. The industry-time effects absorb the direct effect ofPostt.
For each difference-in-differences model, we employ two specifications: one with firm and industry-time fixed effects, and another that adds the following time-varying control variables:Asset Size=Ln(Assets),Ln(Shareholders' Equity),Asset Tangibility= Property, plant, and equipment/Assets), andAsset Turnover= Sales/Assets.21We measure each covariate as of the prior year. Industry-time effects are defined at the three-digit ISIC level. This level of granularity generates 130 distinct industry classifications in our sample. Standard errors are clustered by firm.
Our firm-level sample comes from financial statements data collected by Colombia'sSuperintendencia de Sociedades, a government agency in charge of overseeing privately held corporations.22We also obtain loan issuance for each firm fromBanco de la República, Colombia's central bank. The sample includes annual firm-level data between 2007 and 2014. Focusing on this period allows us to examine firm behavior over the four years before (2007 to 2010) and four years after (2011 to 2014) the tax law change. Our sample consists of firms with sales above COP 15,000 million the year before the shock. We drop utilities (ISIC codes 40 and 41), financial firms (ISIC codes 65 to 67), and firm-year observations with asset growth exceeding 100%, and we require that firms have at least one observation both before and after the shock. The final sample contains 13,886 firm-year observations that correspond to 2,006 distinct firms.23
TableIreports summary statistics. Panel A presents statistics based on all of the data in our regression sample, whereas Panel B divides the sample based on exposure to treatment, comparing means from the pretreatment years. The top section of each panel reports results for firm characteristics (i.e., the time-varying control variables in equations1and2), the middle section for financing outcomes, and the bottom section for investment outcomes. In Panel B, we highlight cases in which means differ significantly between the treated and control samples, based on standardt-tests, with asterisks (Table IA.I in the Internet Appendix provides further details on the sample composition by industry segments at the ISIC section level).
Comparing treated versus the first (nonmatched) control sample, we observe statistically significant differences in some of the control variables, such asAsset SizeandAsset Turnover. In addition, capital expenditures are significantly higher for the control firms. After matching, however, these differences are less pronounced. When we compare the treated group with the propensity-score matched sample, none of the differences is statistically significant. Financial outcomes, in contrast, vary much more between treated and control firms, which is expected because the sample is divided based on the issuance of very short-term debt, which substitutes for both cash and trade credit (recall SectionI). Hence, the levels of cash and accounts payable are both lower during the preperiod for the treated group compared to the control group, because treated firms with access to Treasury Facilities use these facilities rather than cash and/or accounts payable to smooth payments. Long-term bank debt is also higher for the treated firms, reflecting their better access to credit in general. These differences in levels are expected, but it is worth emphasizing that our identification does not rely on comparison across these two sets of firms. Rather, we rely on the differentialwithinfirm effect of the 2011 tax change across firms.
Figure6plots bothγt, which represents the average for control firms, andγt+βt, which represents the average for treated firms. We omitYear2010and its interaction, so 2010 serves as the reference year. As with the regressions, in this graph we include all firms in the bottom two quintiles in the control sample. Figure6shows thatSTDdeclines sharply in 2011, and that this drop persisted over the subsequent three years (this is the sample equivalent of the aggregate effect shown in Figure3). Moreover, there is no evidence of any differential trend between the two groups prior to treatment, consistent with the identification requirements for difference-in-differences estimators.24
TableII, Panel A reports results of the difference-in-differences regression that corresponds to Figure6. We report four base regressions: with and without time-varying control variables for the nonmatched sample (columns (1) and (2)) and the matched sample (columns (4) and (5)). Columns (3) and (4) formally test the parallel trends assumption. These latter two specifications include interactions between the year indicators during the preperiod andTreatment. Consistent with the figure, we find thatSTDdeclines sharply for treated firms after the tax law change but not before. In all six models, the point estimate is large, both statistically and economically. For comparison, treated firms average 0.20 forSTDin the pretreatment period (TableI, Panel B). Hence, their issuance of very short-term bank debt declines by about two-thirds (= 0.123/0.20), based on the difference-in-differences coefficient in column (1). We find no evidence of any preperiod significance of the interaction between the year indicators andTreatment, which means that in line with Figure6, trends are parallel during the years prior to the tax change.
Panel B of TableIIreports results for similar regressions after disaggregating the short-term debt issuance data across maturity bins less than one year. These regressions show a decline in issuance for all maturities less than six months. As expected, the magnitude of the treatment effect declines as maturity increases from the shortest maturity (<30 days) to the 91 to 180-day maturity bin. From six months on, we see small increases in issuance with treatment, suggesting some substitution to longer maturities.
TablesIIIandIVlink the tax shock to other firm financial policies. In particular, TableIIIexamines the effect on the levels of bank debt and cash, and TableIVexamines the effect on trade credit. We interpret the models in these analyses as capturing reduced-form relationships in which the treatment effect reflects changes in firm financial policies induced by the decline in the use ofSTD. In TableIII, Panel A reports results for overall bank debt and cash, and Panel B disaggregates bank debt by maturity (over versus under one year in residual maturity). TableIVreports results for net payables in Panel A, and for gross payables and gross receivables in Panel B. We report results for the same four base specifications as in TableII.
Four dimensions of financial policy change when firms reduce the use ofSTDfor liquidity management. First, firms borrow less from banks overall, although this result loses significance in the matched sample. In the bank debt regressions, the coefficients suggest that treated firms reduce debt by about 1.1% of assets, or about 4% of the pretreatment average (TableIII, Panel A, columns (1) and (2)).
Second, treated firms hold more cash after the tax law change (columns (5) to (8)). Cash increases by about 0.8% of assets, or 20% of the pretreatment mean. The increase in the stock of cash likely occurs because firms are more likely to use direct cash disbursement to make payments after the tax law change as a substitute for payments from Treasury Facilities or lines of credit (although we cannot directly measure these flows of payments). Greater direct payment flows require that firms hold a high buffer stock of cash. Economic and statistical significance is similar for the two control samples.
Third, in Panel B of TableIIIwe find that the declines in bank debt are mitigated by partial substitution into longer term debt. Short-term debt (maturity <1 year) declines by about 2.3% of assets, whereas long-term debt increases by 1.1% to 1.5% of assets. Hence, the decline in overall bank borrowing is offset in part by an increase in maturity—firms substitute into long-term debt because it carries a lower tax burden, but the substitution is incomplete. These results support our identification assumption: the tax shock has a very large negative effect only at the short end of the maturity distribution, which induces firms to lengthen debt maturity. The incomplete substitution suggests that long-term and short-term bank debt serve different roles. Short-term bank loans exist to help firms manage payments and liquidity, whereas long-term debt exists to help them finance long-term investment projects.
Fourth, the tax change has a substantial effect on the use of trade credit. Specifically, TableIV, Panel A shows that net accounts payable increases sharply for treated firms, by 1.9% to 2.3% of assets depending on the specification. This change is large relative to the pretreatment average level of net payables (–8.7% of assets). Panel B shows that the net change is driven by a decline in accounts receivable for treated firms, as the increase in accounts payable is not statistically significant. (As we show below, this baseline model masks a key difference in how trade credit responds to treatment as a function of access to trade credit).
TableVreports results on how investment is affected by changes in the tax cost of using bank liquidity. We run the same four models for each outcome. Panel A considers long-term investment, that is, capital expenditures and investments in R&D and other long-term assets. Panel B considers short-term investment in raw materials and supplies in inventory, as well as in finished goods in inventory. Panel C considers acquisitions.
We find significant declines in both long-term and short-term investment, although not across all dimensions. Capital expenditures decline by 0.5% to 0.6% of assets, or about 15% of the pretreatment investment level. Investment in raw materials in inventory also declines significantly, by about 0.3% of assets. This decline may reflect firms selling off inventory (possibly at a discount) to raise their cash buffer. The other dimensions of investment do not change significantly.
Above we have shown that net accounts payable rises sharply with the tax law change, and we also find declines in both long-term and short-term investment. However, access to trade credit may vary substantially across firms. In particular, better access to accounts payable should reduce firms’ need to increase their cash buffer, while continuing to finance inventories. In our next set of tests, we therefore examine whether firms less able to substitute trade credit for bank credit experience larger real effects when the tax law changes. In answering this question, we separately examine accounts payable (the borrowing side of trade credit) and accounts receivable (the lending side) because frictions should constrain firms only on the borrowing side.
To see why frictions might matter, consider a firm that can wait only 30 days to make payments (without penalty), but that must wait 45 days to receive cash from customers after a sale. A firm such as this needs a bridge to span the 15-day timing mismatch. The bridge could be built either by holding a buffer stock of cash or by borrowing on a credit line or Treasury Facility. Clearly, if the option to access bank credit over very short horizons like 15 days becomes more expensive, the firm would need to increase its holdings of cash, which in turn may displace investment, either because internal funds get diverted away from investment or because holding a higher stock of cash leads to permanently lower investment. Firms in the opposite situation, that is, those with greater access to trade credit, would be less dependent on banks (or cash) for liquidity because of their ability to substitute into trade credit.
Much of the theory of trade credit relies on the idea that suppliers have an informational advantage over other providers of liquidity (i.e., banks), or that suppliers are best able to redeploy collateral. Schwartz (1974) provides an early explanation of trade credit as a key component of a firm's pricing policy, motivated in part by differential access to capital markets. Smith (1987) provides a broad theory of interfirm credit that includes product quality, market power, and sunk costs. She proposes that delayed payment allows buyers to verify product quality before paying. Smith (1987) also models trade credit as a screening contract whereby terms can be designed to provide credit quality information, as the response to credit terms may help identify low-creditworthy buyers. Burkart and Ellingsen (2004) argue that a key advantage for trade credit suppliers is their certainty that the transaction with the buyer has occurred. Overall, such explanations suggest that industry characteristics determine the availability of trade credit. In line with this view, based on data compiled from a survey of credit managers for over 2,000 Compustat firms, Ng, Smith, and Smith (1999) show that there is significant variation in trade credit policiesacrossindustries, whereas there is little variationwithinindustries.
Petersen and Rajan (1997) provide comprehensive empirical evidence correlating accounts payable and accounts receivable to firm characteristics for small U.S. firms. They show that the use of accounts payable of firms whose inventories are finished goods is more than 70% lower than that of firms with no finished goods in inventory. Fisman and Love (2003) report that they find similar correlations based on comparisons across countries and industries. In contrast, Giannetti, Burkhart, and Ellingsen (2011) find that more trade credit is supplied by firms that sell differentiated products. The difficulty with these reduced-form correlations, however, is that they reflect both supply-and demand-side factors. On the trade credit supply side, raw materials are easier to repossess and put into alternative uses. On the demand side, however, trade credit users may have greater demand to delay payments on differentiated goods in order to certify their quality (Smith (1987)).
Our setting allows us to identify a shock that both increases trade credit demand and decreases its supply. This follows because, as we have shown, the tax on short-term bank credit induces treated firms to substitute toward alternative means of payment (i.e., net trade credit increases) but also increases the cost of supplying trade credit to other firms (gross accounts receivable declines). Together these effects imply that the equilibrium price of trade credit should be higher after the tax law change. Figure7supports this prediction: in aggregate, firms exploit early-payment discounts less frequently after the tax change.25
The cross-sectional implication of these effects is that firms with greater access to trade credit should substitute relatively more into accounts payable in response to the negative tax shock, compared to firms with less access to trade credit. To test this implication, we measure trade credit availability in a setting that has not been distorted by either the BAD taxes or the 2011 tax law change on bank loans. Specifically, we construct our measure based on the median Compustat firm in the United States by industry, defined at the three-digit ISIC level. We set the indicatorHigh-TC Accessto one for firms in industries in which the average days to make payments (= Accounts payable/(Cost of goods sold + Change in inventory)) exceeds the average days to receive payments (= Accounts receivable/Sales).26Firms that can wait longer to make payments (relative to when they receive them) are in a better position to substitute trade credit for bank-supplied short-term loans. Murfin and Njoroge (2015) document substantial heterogeneity in this measure by firm size, but our purpose is to avoid variation in access to trade credit due to firm-specific characteristics and to capture the overall availability of trade credit based on exogenous industry characteristics such as the nature of inventories (raw materials versus finished goods) or technology differences.
TablesVIandVIIreport financial outcomes for the extended model. We find that bank debt declines with exposure to the tax shock, again more so in the model without matching. When we split bank debt by maturity, we find declines in short-term debt of similar magnitudes, irrespective ofHigh-TC Access(TableVI, Panel B, columns (1) to (4)). These results support our identification assumption, namely that the tax shock raises the relative price of very short-term bank debt for all treated firms similarly. But the induced effects on theotherfinancial outcomes differ substantially with access to trade credit. Firms withoutHigh-TC Accesssubstitute into long-term debt, while those withHigh-TC Accessdo not (TableVI, Panel B, columns (5) to (8)). Similarly, firms withoutHigh-TC Accesssubstitute into cash, and those withHigh-TC Accessdo not (TableVI, Panel A, columns (5) to (8)).
TableVIIshows that use of trade credit explains the differential effects of the tax law change on cash and long-term debt. OnlyHigh-TC Accessfirms increase their use of accounts payable (both net and gross); for firms withoutHigh-TC Access, accounts receivable declines (recall that the cost of supplying trade credit has increased for all firms), but payables do not increase significantly (in fact, the point estimate is negative). For firms withHigh-TC Access, gross accounts payable increases substantially, by 1.5% to 2.0% of assets (TableVII, Panel B, columns (1) to (4)). Thus, some firms increase their payables—that is, borrow more from suppliers—when short-term bank credit becomes expensive as doing so allows them to manage liquidity without holding more cash. Others, namely those for which trade credit frictions are substantial, instead must increase their buffer stock of cash as it is more expensive to borrow from suppliers than before.
TableVIIIsuggests that the increase in cash for firms withoutHigh-TC Accesshas negative real effects. Investment behavior differs sharply across industries. Firms withoutHigh-TC Accesscut long-term investment, whereas firms withHigh-TC Accessdo not. The magnitude of the decline in Capital Expenditures increases to 1% to 1.3% of assets, or about twice the amount observed in the simple specifications. Investment in R&D also falls significantly only for firms withoutHigh-TC Access(TableVIII, Panel A). Investment rates likely fall because raising the buffer stock of cash to replace short-term bank loans diverts internal funds away from investment (and external funds are costly).28In contrast, firms withHigh-TC Accessare able to increase their use of accounts payable, which limits their need to raise cash and avoids the investment distortions that would otherwise occur. Declines in short-term investments—raw materials in inventory—are somewhat larger for firms withoutHigh-TC Access, but this difference is small and not statistically significant. As in the simpler models, we find no significant change in acquisitions for either type of firm following the tax shock.
article.III. Conclusion:
Bank credit lines and very short-term loans offer liquidity to firms, limiting the need to hold cash. Our results suggest that some firms can avoid holding larger buffers of cash when short-term bank credit becomes more expensive by substituting into trade credit. To identify this substitution, which has been a challenge in the literature, we exploit a tax shock in Colombia that penalizes very short-term bank loans. We show that substitution away from banks and into trade credit is important, as it allows firms to avoid the reductions in real investment that we find in industries where access to trade credit is limited. Trade credit is thus an important alternate source of liquidity that can insulate firms from bank liquidity shocks. Unlike much of the extant literature, we analyze a shock that affects very short-term bank credit used for liquidity without feeding back into the long-term credit role of banks.
Our paper also highlights a pervasive theme in the empirical literature, namely the fundamental tradeoff between tight identification and generalizability, sometimes referred to as external validity. We acknowledge limitations from concerns with respect to external validity, as our tax shock happens in an unusual setting where firms have already adapted to a broad bank transaction tax. Yet the key finding—that trade credit can substitute for bank liquidity and thus mitigate real distortions—is consistent with broader but less well-identified settings. For example, Garcia-Appendini and Montoriol-Garriga (2013) show that trade credit supplied by cash-rich firms increases around the 2008 Financial Crisis. They argue that the increase reflects substitution motivated by the declining supply of liquidity from banks. Our study complements their work because the tax shock in Colombia is very specific—it affectsonlyvery short-term bank loans, leaving other potentially confounding variables like investment demand or changes in the supply of long-term bank loans unaffected (recall Figure4). Our study thus helps mitigate the identification concerns faced by their study, whereas their study helps mitigate the external validity concerns faced by ours.
